## How It Works

Convert stellar parallax measurements (in arcseconds) to distance in parsecs using a simple, direct formula. This calculator applies the standard conversion with your input values and alerts you to unusually large or small measurements.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference for educational purposes. For critical decisions (financial, medical, or legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically for accuracy.

faq.a: The apparent shift in a star's position when observed from opposite sides of Earth's orbit around the sun.

faq.q: How does parallax measure stellar distance?
faq.a: Smaller parallax angles indicate greater distances. The formula d = 1/p (in parsecs) directly converts the parallax angle to distance.
One parsec is the distance at which a star has a parallax angle of 1 arcsecond. It equals 3.26 light-years or 3.09 × 10¹³ km.
With a parallax of 0.77 arcsec, Proxima Centauri is 1.3 pc or 4.24 light-years away—our closest star.

faq.q: What are the limits of the parallax method?
faq.a: Ground-based measurements work well up to ~100 parsecs. The Hipparcos satellite reached this limit; Gaia extends measurements to ~10,000 parsecs.

faq.q: Can you measure parallax for galaxies?
faq.a: No. For distant galaxies, astronomers use Cepheid variable stars or Type Ia supernovae as standard candles.
